title: CHAPTER ONE: THE OPEN GATE
content:
> *"The Baihui acupoint sits at the crown of the skull, where heaven meets the mortal vessel. In most practitioners, this gate admits a trickle of celestial energy sufficient to nourish the upper dantian. In exceedingly rare cases, the gate is born open -- thrown wide like a door ripped from its hinges. Such individuals perceive the flow of qi as others perceive sunlight: effortlessly, constantly, with a brilliance that will eventually blind them."*
>
> -- Yeo Il-shin, Elder of the Southern Edge Sect, private correspondence to the Desolate Sect Archives, Year of the Iron Horse

---

The night the elf came, the river was silver.

Not poetically silver. Actually, physically silver -- moonlight pooling in the shallows of the Bai River until it looked like molten currency poured across the landscape. The kind of night that made even the dogs stop barking.

A man knocked on the gate of the Jeong estate.

The gatekeeper, Old Zhang, had been half asleep on his stool, a clay jug of sorghum wine warming his gut. He lurched upright at the sound and peered through the viewing slot, then promptly dropped his jug. It shattered on the flagstones, and the sharp smell of liquor rose between them like an offering.

The visitor had long ears. Not long like a donkey's -- tapered, leaf-shaped, rising to elegant points above a face so symmetrical it made Zhang's teeth ache. His skin held the faintest luminescence, as if lit from within by a banked hearth fire, and he smelled of grass and rain and something older than both.

An elf. Here. In Xinye County, where the most exotic thing Zhang had seen in forty years was a two-headed carp pulled from the irrigation canal.

"I am a physician," the elf said, in flawless Henan-accented Mandarin that somehow made Zhang feel like an illiterate child. "I require lodging for the evening. In exchange, I will treat any who are ailing."

Zhang ran. Not away from the elf -- toward the main house, sandals slapping courtyard stones, his voice preceding him in a breathless shout that woke three servants and a barn cat. The master had a newborn. The third wife had died in labor six days past, and the child had been screaming since.

A doctor. An *elf* doctor. Zhang would have kissed the heavens if he'd had the reach.

---

Jeong Dae-myeong, master of the Jeong estate, was not a man who showed gratitude easily. Thick-necked, broad-shouldered, with a jaw like a brick. He'd built the family from a minor landholding into the de facto law enforcement of Xinye County through stubbornness, a decent sword arm, and a complete unwillingness to acknowledge that anyone in the room might be smarter than him.

He brought the elf to the nursery himself.

The child was small. Wrinkled and red-faced, fists clenched like tiny war hammers, lungs working overtime to broadcast his displeasure to anyone within earshot. The wet nurse had dark circles under her eyes and a look of quiet desperation that suggested she was reconsidering her career choices.

The elf picked up the baby with practiced ease -- one palm supporting the skull, the other cradling the spine -- and went very still.

His eyes changed. Amber irises brightened, pupils contracting to pinpoints. The candles flickered. A pressure filled the nursery that made Dae-myeong's ears pop. The child stopped crying.

Silence. The kind that has weight.

"Well?" Dae-myeong said. He was not a patient man.

The elf looked at the baby for a long time. When he spoke, his voice had lost its casual warmth.

"It will be difficult for this child to live past twenty. That is the constitution he was born with."

The words landed in the room like a stone dropped into still water. Dae-myeong stared. The wet nurse pressed her knuckles to her mouth.

"An early death," Dae-myeong said. His voice was flat. "This child was born after I disgraced its mother. She died bringing it into the world. Is this divine punishment?"

The elf's expression shifted. It was subtle -- the barest tightening around his mouth, a cooling in his luminous eyes -- but it transformed his face from beautiful to frightening.

"Is *that* what you ask a doctor? Whether the heavens are punishing you through your infant son?" He looked at Dae-myeong the way a scholar looks at a dog that has wandered into a library. "Even for a provincial landowner, that is spectacularly ignorant."

"Watch your tongue--"

"His condition is similar to what your physicians call the Nine Yin Severed Pulse, but inverted. Where that ailment hardens the meridians until energy cannot flow, your son's Baihui acupoint -- here, at the crown -- is catastrophically open." The elf pressed a finger, gentle as a moth's wing, to the soft spot on the baby's skull. "Open like a gate with no door. The energy of heaven and earth pours through him constantly. His upper dantian is flooded with it."

Dae-myeong frowned. He understood perhaps half of this. "The Nine Yin Severed Pulse kills through blockage. You're saying my son will die from... the opposite?"

"The common folk call it ascension. They think it means a Taoist master departing for the celestial realm in a pillar of light." The elf stroked one long ear. "In truth, ascension is what happens when the upper dantian absorbs more energy than the mortal vessel can contain. The consciousness dissolves. The body follows. It is not transcendence. It is drowning in a river you cannot see."

"Then it *is* divine punishment."

"You turtle of a man." The elf's composure cracked, a flash of genuine anger crossing features that were otherwise ageless and serene. "You have a son with a condition rarer than phoenix feathers, and your first instinct is to make it about your own guilt? If this child were raised in Shaolin, the seventy-two arts would double in his hands. If he were trained by the unorthodox sects, he would become a demon that would make the continent tremble. His mind is a vessel built to hold the ocean, and you are asking me whether the ocean is *angry at you*."

"I've heard enough." Dae-myeong stood, color rising in his neck. "I don't care if you're from a noble clan. You are a guest in my home, and your words are--"

"Rank?" The elf's voice dropped to something quiet and cold. "You dare speak to me of rank? Even if your great-grandfather's great-grandfather stood before me, he would not--"

But Dae-myeong was already shouting for Zhang, and within minutes the elf was escorted to the gate with more haste than courtesy. The physician went without resistance, though his expression suggested he was memorizing the experience for future reference in a very unflattering medical journal.

The nursery door closed. The baby began to cry again. Dae-myeong stood over the crib and looked at his third son -- this motherless, allegedly doomed, apparently extraordinary child -- and felt nothing he could name.

He turned and walked away.

The baby screamed into the empty room.

---

Outside the estate walls, the elf paused on the moonlit road.

"If raised in an unorthodox sect, this child would have become a demon," he murmured. "If grown in Shaolin, the seventy-two arts would have doubled. But born as a hated child in this backwater..."

He looked back at the estate. The crying had stopped.

"There is no path for him but to die young. Unless he awakens on his own."

The elf walked south, and was gone.
